title: Present Tense Verb Conjugation - Regular
text:
Approximate time: 30 minutes

Learning Objectives
In this lesson, the learner will:

Conjugate regular verbs in the present tense



Conjugating verbs in the Present tense
Conjugation is how a verb changes based upon the doer of the action. In English, this looks like:

I jump
She jumps

The first-person singular version of “to jump” is jump, while the third-person singular version is jumps. In English, conjugation is usually pretty straightforward in many tenses. So much so, that we forget that we do it. However, many languages, including German, have more complexity in their conjugation and different subjects have different conjugations.
Let’s demonstrate conjugation of a regular verb in German, gehen, which means “to go”:







ich gehe
wir gehen


du gehst
ihr geht


er gehtsie gehtes geht
Sie* gehensie gehen



* Note that the formal Sie follows the conjugation of the third-person, plural, even though it refers to the second-person singular or plural. This will be true in all tenses.
For regular verbs the rule is to remove the -en ending from the infinitive version of the verb and add these endings for a given subject:



-e
-en


-st
-t


-t
-en









Exercise



Let’s introduce a few more common regular verbs in the present tense.



Deutsch
English




machen
to do or to make


spielen
to play


lernen
to learn


fragen
to ask


sagen
to say


kaufen
to buy




Create flashcards to learn these common verbs
Conjugate each of the verbs

Answer Key
Code

Lessons

Nominative pronouns
Present tense verb conjugation - Regular verbs
Present tense verb conjugation - Irregular verbs

title: Nominative Pronouns
text: Approximate time: 30 minutes

title: Nominative Pronouns
text:
Aren’t these just pronouns?
While reading this you might be asking yourself, “Aren’t these just pronouns?” and “What does it mean to specifically be a nominative pronoun?” These are great questions and it is a distinction that we will be making early in these lessons. Let’s consider this sentence in English:

He eats her.

He and her are both pronouns in the above sentence, but He is the subject of the sentence while her is the thing that the verb is acting upon. We don’t say in English:

He eats she.

As you can see in English, our pronouns sometimes change based upon whether we are the doer of the action or the receiver of the action. This difference exists in German as well and is more nuanced. We will discuss this much more in later sections, but this is just to acknowledge that this difference exists so that we can demonstrate that not all pronouns are nominative, even in English.

title: Nominative Pronouns
section: What do we mean by formal and informal?
text:
What do we mean by formal and informal?
In German, when you are addressing certain people, you will use the formal version of you and other times you will use the informal version. Times you would consider using the formal version of you would be:

Speaking to someone you’ve never meet before
Speaking to someone of authority (police, teachers, etc.)
Speaking to someone significantly older than you as a sign of respect

The general rule that I use is:






When to use the formal version of You



If a Southerner would use Sir or Ma’am to address this person, you’re probably using the formal version.



Moving from formal to informal
Some people, like police officers, you will always address with the formal version of You. However, like using Sir or Ma’am, someone may say something like “You don’t need to use Sir/Ma’am with me” and Germans have this same concept, but it is a bit more subtle. The person that commands respect will use the informal du to address you. At which point, you are now in a informal you relationship with this person for the rest of your life. The general rule for this is:






When to transition from formal to informal



Once they use the informal version to address you, then you are now on an informal basis with this person for the rest of your life.




Differentiating between Sie and sie
You might notice from the above table that Sie (formal version of you (singular and plural)) is spelled the same as sie (third-person singular she and plural they). The pronunciation of these is the exact same and requires context to differentiate when speaking. The conjugation of the verb that follows can help with this context.
In written form the major distinction is that the the capitalized Sie refers to the formal version while the lowercase sie refers to either she or they. However, words at the start of a sentence are capitalized like in English and given that these are nominative pronouns, they are usually the subject of the sentence. As a result, it is common to see sie referring to she or they capitalized, but only because they start a sentence and not because they refer to the formal you.


Formal usage moving forward
We will limit the use of the formal you for simplicity moving forward in these lessons, but I will note it were applicable. This decision is to try to streamline examples and because, from my experience, learners oftentimes get a fair bit of latitude on this distinction. German speakers are generally excited to see you trying to speak German and not as focused on these social nuances. That being said, if I ran into a problem and needed help from the police or was meeting my partner’s parents/grandparents for the first time, I would try to give extra special attention toward using the formal version.






Exercise



Make flashcards for these nominative pronouns and practice them until you feel confident with them.

title: Nominative Pronouns
text:
Formals absence from these lessons.
Generally speaking, I will be avoiding

Description
The goal of these materials are to provide a basic understanding of the German language for English-speakers where the explanations for the concepts and sentence structure will be provided in English. I should be clear from the start that my grasp of the German language is incomplete, but I am trying to write up lessons as my knowledge grows.
A major concept for language learning is vocabulary acquisition. In these materials, we are going to attempt to limit the vocabulary and rather focus on how the language itself works. At some points vocabulary will be necessary to progress, but I will be making attempts to minimize this as much as possible.

Learning Objectives
In these lessons, the learner will:

Describe grammar concepts from German
Assemble German sentences

A Note about General Rules
Throughout the materials we will be using “General Rules”. These are rules that hold in many, if not all, circumstances. We will denote them but putting them in a special block like:






How “General Rules” will look



“General Rules” will be in special blocks like this.

Lessons

title: Present Tense Verb Conjugation - Irregular
text:
Approximate time: 45 minutes

Learning Objectives
In this lesson, the learner will:

Conjugate irregular verbs in the present tense



Sein
Sein mean “to be” and it is probably the most difficult verb to conjugate in the present tense because it doesn’t follow any particular rules and you need to just memorize its conjugation. The good news is that you will you use it so frequently that it will become second-nature rather quickly. Below is the conjugation table for Sein:







ich bin
wir sind


du bist
ihr seid


er istsie istes ist
Sie sindsie sind





Infinitive stems ending in -s, -ß, -x or -z
Infinitive verbs whose stem ends in -s, -ß, -x or -z has only uses -t in the for the second-person singular. The rationale I use for this is that these infinitive stems already have either an -s sound at the end of the stem or a sound that is similar to -s. As a result adding an -st ending wouldn’t be heard any different than -t. Some common words that fall into this category are:









-s
-ß
-x
-z




reisen (to travel)
heißen (to be named)
faxen (to fax)
tanzen (to dance)



schließen (to close)

sitzen (to sit)



grüßen (to greet)

besitzen (to own)



This is probably a good place to point out that -s and -ß related. The -ß is sometimes replaced with -ss. As such, rules that apply to -s, will also apply to -ß.
Let’s see an example of one of these conjugations:







ich heiße
wir heißen


du heißt
ihr heißt


er heißtsie heißtes heißt
Sie heißensie heißen





Infinitive stems ending in -n rather than -en
handeln (to trade) erinnern (to remember) ändern (to change) sammeln (to collect) lächeln (to smile) segeln (to sail) wechseln (to change or to exchange)


Infinitive stems ending in -d, -t or if it ends in an -m or -n that is not preceded by an r
Infinitive stems ending in -d, -t or if it ends in an -m or -n that is not preceded by an r have a rather hard sound at the end of the infinitive stem, so we need to have an e added after the infinitive stem but prior to the -st or -t in order to be able to pronounce it. A table of common verbs that fall into this category are below:









-d
-t
-m not preceded by r
-n not preceded by r




baden (to bathe)
arbeiten (to work)
atmen (to breathe)
öffnen (to open)


schneiden (to cut)
antworten (to answer)

rechnen (to calculate)


laden (to load or to charge)
retten (to rescue)

zeichnen (to draw)



beten (to pray)

regnen (to rain)









TipInfinitive stems ending in an -m or -n that is preceded by an r are regular verbs



Just to prove the rule, let’s look at a few infinitive stems that either end in -m or -n and are preceded by and l or an r.
lernen (to learn)







ich lerne
wir lernen


du lernst
ihr lernt


er lerntsie lerntes lernt
Sie lernensie lernen



wärmen (to warm)







ich wärme
wir wärmen


du wärmst
ihr wärmt


er wärmtsie wärmtes wärmt
Sie wärmensie wärmen







Stem-changing verbs
Some verbs change their stem in the second-person singular and the third-person singular. Unfortunately, there aren’t any tricks or mnemonics for recognizing which ones do stem change, which ones don’t. However, the good news is that many are used frequently enough that you will likely learn the most common offenders quickly. There are three classes of stem-changing verbs:

a → ä
e → i
e → ie

Each of the tabsets below provides an example for how each class of these stem-changing verbs is conjugated:

a → äe → ie → ie


laufen (to walk or to run)







ich laufe
wir laufen


du läufst
ihr lauft


er läuftsie läuftes läuft
Sie laufensie laufen





geben (to give)







ich gebe
wir geben


du gibst
ihr gebt


er gibtsie gibtes gibt
Sie gebensie geben





sehen (to see)







ich sehe
wir sehen


du siehst
ihr seht


er siehtsie siehtes sieht
Sie sehensie sehen






More examples of stem-changing verbs from each class can be found in the tabset below:

a → äe → ie → ie



laufen (to walk or to run)
schlafen (to sleep)
fahren (to drive or to travel)
tragen (to wear)
waschen (to wash)
lassen (to let or to leave)
schlagen (to hit)
fallen (to fall)
halten (to stop)




essen (to eat)
geben (to give)
sprechen (to speak)
helfen (to help)
treffen (to meet someone you are familiar with)




sehen (to see)
lesen (to read)
empfehlen (to recommend)
stehlen (to steal)






Other strange conjugations
nehmen haben wissen werden


Impersonal Verbs
There are a handful of verbs that really only exist in the third-person. All of them deal either defining the state of something or are weather-related. People are unable to do these verbs and, therefore, they are referred to as impersonal verbs. Some examples are in the tabset below:

Define the state of somethingWeather-related verbs


Some impersonal verbs which define the status of a thing or an event are:

stattfinden (to happen/to take place)
passiern (to happen)
eilen (to be urgent)
stimmen (to be true)
sich lohnen (to be worth it)



Other impersonal verbs fall within the category of being weather-related, such as:

hageln (to hail)
regnen (to rain)
schneien (to snow)
blitzen* (to lightning)
donnern* (to thunder)

* Note that these verbs in the context of weather, only have third-person conjugations, but they do have alternative meanings that can be conjugated in the first- and second- person. See the collapsible discussion below for more information.



As you can see, these are all verbs that people can’t “do”. You can’t rain, I can’t be true and we can’t happen, but it can hail, be true or happen. These impersonal verbs exist in third-person singular and plural.
You might wonder how the weather-related impersonal verbs can occur in the third-person plural. When they are used in the weather-related context, they are usually referring to the things that are coming from the sky, the hailstones, raindrops, snowflakes, etcetera. For example:

Die Hagelkörner hageln gegen das Fenster. - The hailstones are hailing against the window.
Flocken schneien vom Himmel. - The snowflakes are snowing from the sky.
Regenschauer regnen auf die Stadt nieder. - Rain showers are raining down on the city.

However, in most instances you’ll run into these weather-related impersonal verbs in the third-person singular:

Es hagelt. - It is hailing.
Es schneit. - It is snowing.
Es regnet - It is raining







NotePrefixes with schneien, which change the meaning





There are separable prefixes that can be added to schneien (rein‑ / herein‑ / vorbei‑), which change the meaning from “to snow” to “to stop by (casually/unexpectedly)”. As a result, these can exist in first- and second-person and you may seen them in a sentence like:

Ich schneie später rein. - I am stopping by later.










NoteAdditional definitions for blitzen and donnern





Since blitzen (to lightning) and donnern (to thunder) are weather-related verbs, you may be tempted to not concern yourself with them in the first- and second-person. And in the context of weather-related events, they do act as impersonal verbs since we can’t lightning or you can’t thunder (in the weather sense). However, each of these verbs have a secondary definition that can be used in the first- and second-person without any prefixes (unlike schneien; see the collapsible discussion above). For blitzen, it can refer to flashing one’s eyes. For donnern, like English, it can also mean to make loud noises. The takeaway message is that not all weather-related verbs fail to have first- and second-person conjugations. But it is true that these weather-related verbs act as impersonal verbs when discussing weather. However, they aren’t impersonal verbs for other definitions.









TipExercise



Answer Key

section: Infinitive stems ending in an -m or -n that is preceded by and l or an r are regular verbs
text:
